So this method should never really be called directly by a user or a developer creating a skill, but rather it is invoked by `_flask_view_func`. Additionally, `_map_intent_to_view_func` will only be called from `_flask_view_func` if the incoming request is of "IntentRequest" type.

You can picture a general outline of flask-ask as `User Speaks` -> `Alexa sends request to flask-ask` -> `Process request within _flask_view_func` -> `invoke user defined functions`.  So the user isn't actually responsible for providing the intent name, rather `_map_intent_to_view_func` is called IF there is an intent name.

Therefore, the intent parameter will always be present during this method call and this method will only be called if there is an intent in the request.

That being said, it doesn't necessarily hurt to provide a way to catch an exception. However when using except clauses you always want to specify the type of `Exception` you are checking for, otherwise your remedy may be trying to fix a different problem than what actually occurred. A blanket except statement also isn't able to provide the user/developer with useful information.

Also, placement of the try/except clauses should target a single source of an error. With this code block you are trying to look up a view function from a dictionary, inspect the arguments of the function, and then calling a whole other function using both the intent and argument names. There are quite a few exceptions that could occur there, but we wouldn't know what caused the error, especially if no specific `Exception` is used in the statement.

Perhaps a more useful error-handling would look something like:
```
try:
    view_func = self._intent_view_funcs[intent.name]

# "intent" object has no name attribrute, this is unlikely to occur
except AttribruteError as e:  
    _dbgdump('The IntentRequest from the Alexa Service was malformed and does not include a name for the intent.')
    return None


 # No view func is stored in self._intent_view_funcs for the given intent name
except KeyError as e: 
    _dbgdump('No intent view function found for Intent {}.' .format(intent.name)
    return None # will result in a 404 Not Found response

argspec = inspect.getargspec(view_func)
arg_names = argspec.args
arg_values = self._map_params_to_view_args(intent.name, arg_names)
return partial(view_func, *arg_values)
```

I would suggest to split ``incoming_response`` and ``swagger_result`` evaluation.

Let's assume that the service returns HTTP/503 from the request (the exception will be part of ``exceptions_to_catch``).
If you're extracting both objects together you'll not be able to keep trace of ``incoming_response`` as the evaluation of ``swagger_result`` calls ``unmarshal_response`` that will raise the expected bravado exception.
So this method call will return a ``BravadoResponse`` with no information about the incoming response.

My suggestion would be to split them
```python
try:
    incoming_response = self._get_incoming_response(timeout=timeout)
    swagger_result = xxx  # If this raises incoming_response has already be assigned and will be available
    ....
except exceptions_to_catch as e:
    ... 
```

Hmm so I've been thinking about this some more. A plain `.retry()` won't use the retry/jitter from the `retryable_task` I think? And as such will use the defaults of "wait 3 minutes" and will also retry indefinitely:
http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/tasks.html#retrying

What behaviour is appropriate here? I think perhaps we should stop retrying at some point, at least so invalid data submissions don't get stuck.

Another option might be to modify `retryable_task` such that the try-except has another case for `MissingResultsetException`, where we don't use the `.record_exception()` thus avoiding the New Relic noise, but still keeping the rest of the `retryable_task` benefits.

I changed my code a few minutes before this to do this slightly differently since it's best not to modify members in another module. See the updated version [here](https://github.com/ray-project/cloudpickle/blob/982da06bb13c330bac3e0a55fabd319778bd0c4c/cloudpickle/cloudpickle.py#L82).
(Sorry for the comment repost, GitHub deleted two comments instead of one.)

In cloudpickle we have to stay 100% pure python (hence this hack in ctypes). Furthermore we have to respect the pickle protocol and this function is called by the load function when hitting the REDUCE opcode. At this point the `bytes` buffer has already been read and is poped from the unpickler stack, but it's still in the lexical scope of the caller function (`load_reduce` IIRC).

However this bytes object will never be used anywhere else, so I am still quite confident that this ctypes hack, when called in the context of the unpickler is "safe".

PIPE enables the stdout and stderr to be retrieved in the communicate() call later on.  If we used sys.stdout and sys.stderr then anything output by FFmpeg would instead end up in the actual calling code's stdout and stderr.  It seemed better to capture that output ourselves so we can give it more context (i.e. labeling it as coming from FFmpeg) and route it through the logging system.

Strictly speaking we're only using the stderr result so stdout could alternatively be assigned to a devnull file like I do in available(), but we wouldn't be able to use a context manager for the devnull file since it needs to stay open until the VideoOutput itself is closed, which just makes it another resource to manage, so using PIPE seemed easier even though we don't recover that data later.  Note that I don't actually think FFmpeg typically writes anything at all to stdout.

Let's do it with `count`... Unless I measured something wrong it's a lot faster:

```
import timeit

setup = '''
attrValue = '\'\"Where\'s www.wikipedia.org?\"\''
'''

a = '''
quot_count = 0
apos_count = 0

for c in attrValue:
    if c == '"':
        quot_count += 1
    elif c == "'":
        apos_count += 1

quote = "'" if quot_count > apos_count else '"'
'''

b = '''
quot_count = 0
apos_count = 0

quot_count = attrValue.count("'")
apos_count = attrValue.count('"')

quote = "'" if quot_count > apos_count else '"'
'''

number = 1000000
print(timeit.timeit(stmt=a, setup=setup, number=number))
print(timeit.timeit(stmt=b, setup=setup, number=number))
```

In the original implementation, we only pass one unique_ptr, so the caller (`cpp2py`) and the callee (`Optimize`) share the same instance. And in `Optimize` (after the std::move(proto)), the callee own the `proto`. When `Optimize` finish, the proto will be released.

In the proposed one, we pretty much do the same thing. The only difference is we transfer the ownership to the parameter of `Optimize`. So the first parameter of `Optimize` owns the proto. So the life scope of the proto ends at the end of `Optimize` function.

So I don't see big win here.

Cool, fair enough. This comes up all over the library, actually. e.g. https://github.com/seequent/properties/blob/v0.5.0b1/properties/basic.py#L627 and I agree it's not very pythonic.

My only reasoning for this is that properties not only provides pretty strict type-checking and validation for users of the libraries, but also for developers building HasProperties classes. Especially in the HasProperties metaclass, there is some fairly strict micromanaging. Requiring `bool` encourages building the classes a specific way and gives a nice error otherwise.

Despite that reasoning, I think you are right, and remaining Pythonic should rule. Developers should be reading the docs, etc, and added flexibility but slightly-harder-to-follow errors is the right choice.
